one_line_takeaway: By splitting the agent into a trusted-only planner and an untrusted-capable executor mediated by an IFC security monitor, the f-secure LLM system provably blocks indirect-prompt-injection trace hijacking, driving InjectAgent attack success to 0% across four LLMs with negligible overhead and no functionality loss.
key_finding: The f-secure LLM system reduced Attack Success Rate to 0% on InjectAgent across ALL four backbone models and BOTH base and enhanced settings, whereas the vanilla ReAct baseline was broadly vulnerable (e.g., GPT-3.5 Turbo 51.6% base / 67.4% enhanced; GPT-4 Turbo 28.7% / 44.5%). Crucially this security came without sacrificing functionality: step and overall accuracy were maintained or improved versus the vanilla system, and it substantially beat SecGPT (e.g., GPT-3.5 multiple-tool: SecGPT 26.44% step / 0% overall vs f-secure ~96% step / 60% overall). Overhead from the security machinery is negligible — the security/syntax check plus step modification cost about 0.0001x the step-generation LLM cost — and facility execution was ~10x faster than both baselines (attributed to avoiding LangChain's agent-chain execution). The authors credit the accuracy gains partly to SEPF helping the LLM plan more reliably.
core_contribution: The paper proposes the 'f-secure LLM system', a system-level (not model-level) defense against indirect prompt injection built on information flow control (IFC). Its central idea is to disaggregate the two roles an LLM plays in an agentic system: the LLM-based PLANNER (which generates execution steps and may see ONLY trusted information) and a RULE-BASED EXECUTOR (which actually runs tool/facility calls and may touch untrusted data). A SECURITY MONITOR sits between them, filtering intermediate outputs via integrity labels so that untrusted data can never (directly or indirectly) influence the planner. Concretely the authors contribute: (1) a fine-grained integrity security-label model with a lattice/join derivation for combined data; (2) SEPF (Structured Executable Planning Format), a 5-field JSON step format (Index, Instruction, Object, Data_input, Data_output) that makes steps machine-checkable and lets data be passed by reference '{Data_output:i}' rather than inlined; (3) a Context-Aware Working Pipeline coordinating planner P, executor G, and security monitor SM across six stages; and (4) a formal security notion, 'iota-execution trace non-compromise', adapted from IFC noninterference, with a theorem (6.2) proving the f-secure system enforces it while a vanilla LLM system does not. This is the paper's OWN contribution; IFC noninterference and SecGPT are prior work it builds on / compares against, not its inventions.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against agentic LLM systems. The attacker can manipulate any UNTRUSTED information source consumed during query execution (e.g., email bodies, webpage/file content fetched by tools) and embed malicious instructions there. The attacker CANNOT access or control the system itself (no editing source code, configs, or the security-label configuration) and cannot modify the principal's original query. Principals A and non-model facilities T are assumed trusted and uncompromised; inter-object channels are assumed secure (TLS). LLMs are trusted when fed only trusted input but assumed arbitrarily compromisable once they ingest untrusted input (black-box, worst-case assumption). Attacker goal: hijack the execution trace pi_q of a query (get an attacker-chosen step generated), i.e., 'execution trace compromise'. Explicitly OUT OF SCOPE: vulnerabilities inside facilities T; model-level jailbreak/alignment-bypass attacks; channel compromise; and availability attacks. Confidentiality is out of scope too — the label model targets INTEGRITY / executable semantics only.
limitations: Grounded in the paper's own text/tables: (1) With weaker backbones (GPT-3.5 Turbo) the f-secure system's Step Accuracy is consistently higher than its Overall Accuracy across all benchmarks (e.g., single-tool 96.75% step but only 75% overall; multiple-tool 96.45% step but 60% overall), meaning it plans correct individual steps but struggles to chain them into a complete correct result — functionality depends on backbone capability. (2) Security rests entirely on correct integrity labeling driven by the principal's security configuration; a source the principal mislabels as trusted (or a legitimately trusted source that itself relays injected content) falls outside the guarantee. (3) The formal guarantee covers only execution-trace INTEGRITY — confidentiality, facility (tool T) vulnerabilities, model-level jailbreaks, channel compromise, and availability attacks are explicitly excluded, so a compromised executor step can still leak/act on data even though the plan stays attacker-free. (4) The defense requires restructuring the agent into the SEPF/disaggregated pipeline (planner cannot inline untrusted data), a non-trivial architectural constraint versus a drop-in guard. (5) Evaluation tool outputs in the InjectAgent batch were simulated (benchmark provides no real tools) and all treated as untrusted.

datasets_used: InjectAgent benchmark (indirect prompt injection; direct-harm and data-stealing attacks, base and enhanced settings)
Three hand-constructed case studies (one-step, chain-based, conditional compromise) implemented with LangChain Gmail Toolkit and File System Toolkit
quantitative_results: f-secure LLM system: 0% Attack Success Rate on InjectAgent for all 4 models in both base and enhanced settings
Vanilla ReAct ASR, GPT-3.5 Turbo: 51.6% base total (61.0% direct harm / 43.1% data stealing), 67.4% enhanced total (82.0% / 55.3%)
Vanilla ReAct ASR, GPT-4 Turbo: 28.7% base total (18.4% / 38.2%), 44.5% enhanced total (32.2% / 56.0%)
Vanilla ReAct ASR, Gemini-1.5-pro: 20.9% base total (8.8% / 32.2%), 15.1% enhanced total (10.0% / 19.8%)
Vanilla ReAct ASR, Claude-3.5-Sonnet: 17.4% base total (7.5% / 26.2%), 1.1% enhanced total (2.3% / 0%)
Functionality, GPT-3.5 Turbo multiple-tool: SecGPT 26.44% Step Acc / 0% Overall Acc vs f-secure 96.45% Step Acc / 60% Overall Acc (text states ~96.14% step)
Functionality, GPT-3.5 Turbo single-tool: vanilla 100% step / 25% overall, SecGPT 50.89% / 25%, f-secure 96.75% / 75%
Functionality, GPT-3.5 Turbo relation data: vanilla 74.92% / 71.42%, SecGPT 46.34% / 42.58%, f-secure 89.68% / 71.42%
Functionality, Gemini-1.5-pro multiple-tool: vanilla 90.47% / 9.52%, SecGPT 68.21% / 50%, f-secure 97% / 95%
Overhead: security & syntax check plus step modification cost ~0.0001x the step-generation LLM cost; f-secure facility/step execution ~10x faster than vanilla and SecGPT
Case studies used GPT-4 Turbo as the backbone across all three cases, compared against SecGPT
baselines_compared: Vanilla ReAct-based LLM system (implemented with LangChain)
